---
title: "Replication of Card and Krueger (1994)"
subtitle: "Minimum Wages and Employment"
author: "Lele Liao"
date: today
---
---

## Introduction

Does raising the minimum wage reduce employment? For decades, the standard answer
in economics was an unambiguous yes. If labor becomes more expensive, the
textbook model of supply and demand predicts that firms will hire fewer workers.
Card and Krueger (1994) challenged this consensus directly, using a real-world
policy change to test whether the prediction held up in practice.

Their setting is a natural experiment that unfolded in April 1992, when New Jersey
raised its minimum wage from \$4.25 to \$5.05 per hour — one of the largest
state-level increases at the time. Pennsylvania, a neighboring state with a
similar labor market, made no such change. By comparing fast-food employment in
the two states before and after the New Jersey increase, Card and Krueger
isolate the effect of the minimum wage from broader economic trends that would
have affected both states equally.

The method they use is **difference-in-differences (DiD)**. The core idea is
simple: rather than asking how employment changed in New Jersey alone (which
would confuse the policy effect with any other changes happening at the same
time), DiD subtracts the contemporaneous change in Pennsylvania — the control
group — from the change in New Jersey. What remains is an estimate of the
employment effect attributable specifically to the minimum wage increase,
provided that New Jersey and Pennsylvania would have followed similar employment
trends in the absence of the policy. This "parallel trends" assumption is the
key identifying assumption of the DiD design, and the authors argue it is
credible given how similar the two states' fast-food labor markets were in the
period leading up to the change.

---

## Main Analysis

The paper's main outcome variable is full-time equivalent (FTE) employment,
defined as the number of full-time workers plus one half of part-time workers.
This measure captures total labor input more accurately than a simple headcount,
since fast-food restaurants rely heavily on part-time staff.
I replicate the main result of the paper using the original dataset of fast-food restaurants.

First, I calculate employment using full-time equivalent (FTE):

* FTE = full-time workers + 0.5 × part-time workers

Then I compare employment before and after the policy.

### Results

* New Jersey employment change: **+0.51**
* Pennsylvania employment change: **−2.41**
* Difference-in-differences (DID): **+2.91**

![](/images/Research1.png)

The figure shows that employment increased in New Jersey but decreased in Pennsylvania.
This creates a positive difference-in-differences estimate.

### Interpretation

This result is surprising.

According to standard theory, employment should decrease. However, the data shows the opposite:

> **The minimum wage increase did not reduce employment. It may have increased it.**

I also run a simple regression, and the coefficient is about **2.94**, which is very close to the DID estimate. This shows the result is consistent and robust.

The positive DiD estimate is striking precisely because it runs counter to the
standard theoretical prediction. One possible explanation is that fast-food
labor markets are not perfectly competitive: employers may have some degree of
**monopsony power**, meaning they face an upward-sloping labor supply curve
rather than a perfectly elastic one. In such a setting, a minimum wage can
actually increase both wages and employment simultaneously, up to a point —
a result that the simple competitive model rules out by construction. Card and
Krueger's finding therefore does not necessarily refute economic theory; rather,
it suggests that the competitive model may be the wrong model for this
particular market.

---

## Something Additional

### Wage Changes

To confirm that the policy actually worked, I look at wages:

* Low-wage stores increased wages by about **0.81 (around 10%)**
* High-wage stores had almost no change

This shows that the minimum wage policy was enforced.

---

### Price Changes

I also analyze prices:

* New Jersey prices increased by **2.60%**
* Pennsylvania prices decreased by **0.44%**

This suggests that firms passed some of the higher labor costs to consumers.

---

### Wage Distribution

The histogram shows a clear change:

![](/images/Research2.png)

* Before the policy, wages were spread out
* After the policy, many wages are clustered at **$5.05**

This confirms that the minimum wage became binding.

---

### Why DID Works

The DID method works because it compares two groups:

* New Jersey (treatment group)
* Pennsylvania (control group)

It removes:

* Differences between states
* Common time trends

The key assumption is that both states would have followed similar trends without the policy. My results support this assumption.

---

## Conclusion

This replication confirms the headline result of Card and Krueger (1994):
raising the minimum wage in New Jersey did not reduce fast-food employment
relative to Pennsylvania. The DiD estimate is positive and economically
meaningful, and it is robust to both the simple two-by-two calculation and a
regression-based approach.

The paper's contribution goes beyond its specific findings. By bringing
credible causal identification — in the form of a natural experiment and a
difference-in-differences design — to a question that had previously been
studied almost exclusively with cross-sectional data, Card and Krueger helped
shift the empirical standards of labor economics. Their work prompted a broader
rethinking of the competitive labor market model and opened the door to a large
subsequent literature using quasi-experimental methods to study the employment
effects of minimum wages. The debate they sparked is still very much alive
today, but the methodological template they established has become a cornerstone
of modern applied economics.
